transcript: Rajesh: Alright everyone, let's get Sprint 24 priorities aligned. We have a lot on our plates this sprint and I want to make sure we're all focused on the right things. The payment gateway issue is our number one priority right now - Priya, where are we on that? Priya: I've identified the root cause. The webhook signature validation in v3.12.1 is using a stale API secret because the config refresh wasn't included in the deployment manifest. I have a fix ready but I need to test it against the Stripe sandbox before deploying to production. I should have it in staging by end of day. Rajesh: Good. Second priority is the security CVEs - we're on a 48-hour clock on those and the CISO is watching. Priya, I know you're stretched thin, can Daniel help with the CVE patches? Daniel: I can pick up the jackson-databind CVE since I'm familiar with our serialization layer. But I also need to wrap up the auth timeout bug - users are still getting logged out randomly. Rajesh: Fair point. Let's have Daniel fix the auth timeout first since it's a quick win, then shift to helping with CVEs. Maria, how's the database migration looking? Maria: The migration scripts are written but I'm blocked on code review for PR #1855. It's 1,200 lines and nobody's picked it up in two days. I also want to flag that the connection pool exhaustion issue in order-service is related to my migration prep work - if we don't fix the connection leak first, the migration could make things worse. Rajesh: Sam, can you review Maria's PR today? And Maria, prioritize the connection pool fix before the migration. Sam: Will do, but I also just got paged about the SSL certificate expiring in 48 hours. The cert-manager is broken after our DNS migration to CloudFlare. That's a ticking time bomb. Rajesh: That's critical - if that cert expires, everything goes down. Sam, handle the SSL cert first, then review Maria's PR. Jordan, how's the dashboard redesign coming? Jordan: I'm about 60% through the component migration. The real-time WebSocket integration is working but I need the API rate limiting in place before I can connect it to production data streams. Alex, any update on that? Alex: I haven't started the rate limiting yet because I've been pulled into the search performance regression. Three enterprise clients are complaining and Carlos just forwarded me an angry email from Globex. Rajesh: Okay, let's be realistic. Alex, focus on the search regression first since it's impacting revenue. The rate limiting can slip to early next week. Let's reconvene Wednesday to check progress on all fronts.

meeting_title: Architecture Review - API Rate Limiting Design
transcript: Alex: Thanks for joining. I want to walk through my proposed design for the API rate limiting before I start implementation. I'm proposing a token bucket algorithm backed by Redis, with per-API-key configurable limits. Priya: Makes sense. What happens when Redis is unavailable? Do we fail open or fail closed? Alex: Good question. My initial thought was fail open - if Redis is down, we allow all requests through rather than blocking legitimate traffic. Priya: I'd push back on that. If Redis goes down during a DDoS attack, we'd have zero protection. Can we implement a local in-memory fallback with a sliding window counter? Alex: That's a great idea. I'll add a local cache fallback that kicks in when Redis is unreachable. It won't be perfectly synchronized across nodes but it's better than nothing. Maria: How does this interact with the multi-tenant architecture we're building for v2.0? We need rate limits to be tenant-aware, not just API-key-aware. Alex: Right now the design is per API key. I'll need to add a tenant dimension to the rate limit key structure. Something like 'ratelimit:{tenant_id}:{api_key}:{endpoint}'. Maria: Perfect. Also, make sure the Redis keys have proper TTL set so we don't accumulate stale data. We already have the connection pool issue. Sam: On the infrastructure side, should we use the existing Redis cluster or provision a dedicated instance for rate limiting? I'm concerned about the additional load on our session store Redis, especially given the auth timeout issues we're seeing. Alex: Separate instance for sure. Rate limiting will generate a high volume of small reads and writes - we don't want that competing with session management. Sam: Agreed. I'll provision a new Redis cluster with 3 nodes. Alex, can you also make sure the implementation returns standard rate limit headers? We need X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset, and Retry-After for 429 responses. Alex: Already planned. I'll follow RFC 6585 for the 429 response format. Let me also add rate limit metrics to our Prometheus dashboards so we can monitor usage patterns. I'll create a Grafana dashboard showing rate limit hits by tier, endpoint, and tenant. Sam, I'll need you to add the Prometheus scrape config for the new metrics endpoint. Sam: Will do. Add it to the infrastructure ticket and I'll include it in the next Terraform apply.

Maria: Let's do the retrospective for the order-service outage that happened on June 17th. Timeline: at 14:23 UTC, PagerDuty fired alerts for order-service pod restarts. By 14:31, the customer impact was confirmed - order submissions were failing with 504 Gateway Timeout. The incident lasted 47 minutes until we manually scaled the deployment to 6 replicas at 15:10 UTC. Root cause was the connection pool exhaustion I mentioned - the bulk import feature is leaking database connections. Priya: What was the detection gap? Our monitoring should have caught the connection pool saturation before it caused pod restarts. Maria: That's a good point. We have metrics for active connections but no alert threshold set. Action item: we need to add a PagerDuty alert when HikariCP active connections exceed 80% of the pool size. Sam, can you add that to the monitoring stack? Sam: Yes, I'll create the alert rule in Prometheus. I'll set it at 40 out of 50 connections with a 5-minute sustained window to avoid false positives. Daniel: I noticed during the incident that our runbook for database connection issues was last updated 8 months ago and didn't include the new bulk import service. Someone needs to update that runbook. Maria: I'll update the runbook as part of my connection pool fix PR. Another action item from this: we need circuit breakers between the bulk import service and the core order processing pipeline. Right now a slow import can starve the connection pool for regular order submissions. Priya: Agreed. We should also add connection pool metrics to the service health check endpoint so the load balancer can route traffic away from pods with saturated pools. Maria: Good idea. I'll add that to the PR as well. Last thing - Sam, can we increase the connection pool size from 50 to 75 as a temporary measure until the leak is fixed? Sam: I can, but that's just delaying the OOM kill. The pods will still run out of memory eventually. Let's fix the leak first. Maria: Fair enough. Let's target having the connection pool fix merged and deployed by Wednesday.

meeting_title: Product Roadmap Review - Q3 Dashboard & Analytics
transcript: Lisa: Let's review where we are on the dashboard redesign and what needs to happen for the Q3 launch. Jordan, can you give us a status update? Jordan: Sure. I've completed the migration of 8 out of 14 React components to the new design system. The chart library swap from Chart.js to Recharts is done and looking great. The main things remaining are the filter panel with saved views, the real-time WebSocket integration, and the accessibility audit for WCAG 2.1 AA compliance. Lisa: The Acme Corp demo is June 23rd and I really need the dashboard to look polished. What can realistically be ready by then? Jordan: The charts and basic layout will be solid. The filter panel will have core functionality but saved views won't be ready. Real-time data depends on Alex's rate limiting work, which I heard is being deprioritized for the search fix. Alex: Yeah, I have to focus on the Elasticsearch performance regression first. But Jordan, you can mock the real-time feed for the demo using our WebSocket staging server - it has synthetic data that looks realistic enough. Jordan: Good idea. I'll set that up. Lisa, I'll also need access to the Figma designs for the mobile-responsive breakpoints. The current specs only cover desktop. Lisa: I'll share those tomorrow morning. Rajesh, from an engineering perspective, what's the realistic timeline for the full dashboard launch? Rajesh: If we stay on track, the dashboard can be feature-complete by end of Sprint 25, which is July 3rd. But we need to factor in the accessibility audit - Jordan, can you run the automated a11y tests this week and flag any major issues early? Jordan: Will do. I'll run axe-core and Lighthouse accessibility audits on Friday and share the results. Lisa: One more thing - we need to update the product documentation and create a migration guide for customers currently using the legacy dashboard. The old dashboard will be deprecated in Q4. Jordan, can you work with the docs team on that after the redesign ships? Jordan: Sure, I'll coordinate with them. But that should be a separate ticket - TASK-109 already covers the API docs update, we need a new ticket for the dashboard-specific documentation. Lisa: Agreed. I'll create that ticket after this meeting.
